solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int maxPalindromes(string s, int k) {
        int n = s.size();
        vector<vector<bool>> isPalindrome(n, vector<bool>(n, false));
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            isPalindrome[i][i] = true;
            if (i < n - 1) {
                isPalindrome[i][i + 1] = (s[i] == s[i + 1]);
            }
        }
        for (int len = 3; len <= n; ++len) {
            for (int i = 0; i + len - 1 < n; ++i) {
                int j = i + len - 1;
                isPalindrome[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j] && isPalindrome[i + 1][j - 1]);
            }
        }

        vector<int> dp(n + 1, 0);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            dp[i + 1] = dp[i];
            for (int l = i - k + 1; l >= 0; --l) {
                if (isPalindrome[l][i]) {
                    dp[i + 1] = max(dp[i + 1], dp[l] + 1);
                }
            }
        }

        return dp[n];
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n^2)
space complexity: O(n^2)
energy implications: MEDIUM
explanation: The solution precomputes all palindromic substrings using dynamic programming and then uses another DP array to find the maximum number of non-overlapping palindromic substrings of length at least k. The precomputation ensures efficient palindrome checks, and the DP approach ensures optimal selection. The main strength is the efficient use of DP for both palindrome detection and selection, while the main weakness is the O(n^2) space and time complexity, which may be suboptimal for very large strings.
